title: Stress and strain
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master stress, strain and the Young modulus for Cambridge 9702. Clear explanations, worked examples and exam tips for P1 and P2.
learningObjectives: Define tensile stress and tensile strain, and calculate each from given data including cross-sectional area, force and extension
Define the Young modulus and use the relationship E = stress/strain to solve problems involving wires and rods
Describe and interpret a stress–strain graph, identifying the limit of proportionality, elastic limit, yield point and ultimate tensile stress

simpleExplanation.title: Stress & strain in 60 seconds
simpleExplanation.summary: Stress tells you how hard a material is being pulled per unit area, while strain tells you how much it has stretched relative to its original length. The Young modulus connects the two and is a fixed property of the material itself — not the size or shape of the sample.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of stretching a rubber band. Stress is like how hard you're pulling divided by how thick the band is; strain is how much longer it's got compared with its original length. A thicker band of the same rubber needs more force to reach the same strain — that's why we use stress instead of force alone.
steps: Learn the three definitions: stress = F/A, strain = x/L, Young modulus E = stress/strain
Practise converting units — stress is in Pa (N m⁻²), strain is dimensionless, E is in Pa
Sketch a stress–strain graph from memory and label the limit of proportionality, elastic limit, yield point and UTS
Work through at least two numerical problems calculating E from wire data, checking your significant figures and units

sections.content: When a material is stretched or compressed, two questions matter: how large is the internal force per unit area, and by how much has the material deformed relative to its original size? These are answered by **tensile stress** and **tensile strain** respectively. Together they define the **Young modulus** — one of the most tested quantities in Cambridge 9702 Paper 2. Get these three definitions sharp and the rest of the topic falls into place.

Tensile stress (symbol σ, pronounced 'sigma') is defined as the force applied perpendicular to a cross-section divided by the area of that cross-section. It measures how intensely the material is being loaded internally. The SI unit is the pascal (Pa), where 1 Pa = 1 N m⁻². For a wire or rod of uniform circular cross-section, the area A = π r², where r is the radius — be careful not to use the diameter directly. Stress is a scalar quantity in this context; sign conventions (tensile positive, compressive negative) are not required at AS level but are good practice.
σ = F / A     [Pa or N m⁻²]

Tensile strain (symbol ε, pronounced 'epsilon') is the extension of the material divided by its original, unloaded length. Because it is a ratio of two lengths, strain has no units — it is dimensionless. Examiners sometimes award a mark specifically for stating that strain has no units, so it is worth remembering. Strain is often a very small number (e.g. 2 × 10⁻³ for a metal wire), so do not be alarmed by tiny values.

The **Young modulus** (symbol E) is defined as the ratio of tensile stress to tensile strain, provided the material obeys Hooke's law (i.e. we are within the limit of proportionality). It is a material property — it does not depend on the dimensions of the sample. Steel always has a Young modulus of approximately 200 GPa regardless of whether the wire is thick or thin, long or short. A higher Young modulus means a stiffer material that requires greater stress to produce the same strain. The unit of E is the pascal (Pa), since strain is dimensionless.
E = σ / ε = (F × L₀) / (A × x)     [Pa or N m⁻²]

items: Stress = force ÷ cross-sectional area (σ = F/A); always use the area perpendicular to the applied force, calculated as πr² for a wire — never πd².
Strain = extension ÷ original length (ε = x/L₀); it is dimensionless and typically a value much less than 1 for metals.
Young modulus E = stress/strain; it is a property of the material, not the sample, and its unit is Pa (same as stress, because strain has no unit).

The limit of proportionality is the point beyond which stress is no longer directly proportional to strain — the graph curves. The elastic limit is where permanent deformation first occurs; these are distinct points.
On a stress–strain graph, the gradient of the linear region equals the Young modulus; the area under the entire curve to fracture represents the energy absorbed per unit volume (toughness).
For Paper 1, watch for distractors that confuse force with stress, or extension with strain — always check whether area and original length have been divided through.

sections.content: A typical stress–strain graph for a ductile metal such as mild steel passes through several distinct regions. In the **linear (Hooke's law) region**, stress and strain are directly proportional; the gradient equals E. At the **limit of proportionality (P)**, the graph begins to curve but the material is still elastic. At the **elastic limit (E)**, permanent (plastic) deformation begins — if the load is removed, the material does not return to its original length. The **yield point** (sometimes split into upper and lower yield points for steel) is where a large increase in strain occurs with little or no increase in stress; dislocations in the crystal structure are moving rapidly. The **ultimate tensile stress (UTS)** is the maximum stress the material can withstand. Beyond this, **necking** occurs and the material fractures. Brittle materials (e.g. glass, ceramic) show a short linear region and fracture at or near the limit of proportionality with very little plastic deformation.

sections.question: A steel wire of diameter 0.80 mm and original length 2.5 m is stretched by a tensile force of 35 N. The Young modulus of steel is 2.0 × 10¹¹ Pa. Calculate (a) the tensile stress in the wire, (b) the tensile strain, and (c) the extension produced.
sections.solution:
Step 1 — Find the cross-sectional area.
Diameter = 0.80 mm = 0.80 × 10⁻³ m, so radius r = 0.40 × 10⁻³ m.
A = π r² = π × (0.40 × 10⁻³)² = π × 1.6 × 10⁻⁷ = 5.027 × 10⁻⁷ m²

Step 2 — Calculate tensile stress (part a).
σ = F / A = 35 / (5.027 × 10⁻⁷) = 6.96 × 10⁷ Pa ≈ 7.0 × 10⁷ Pa

Step 3 — Calculate tensile strain (part b).
ε = σ / E = (6.96 × 10⁷) / (2.0 × 10¹¹) = 3.48 × 10⁻⁴ ≈ 3.5 × 10⁻⁴ (no units)

Step 4 — Calculate the extension (part c).
ε = x / L₀  →  x = ε × L₀ = 3.48 × 10⁻⁴ × 2.5 = 8.7 × 10⁻⁴ m (= 0.87 mm)

Final answers: σ ≈ 7.0 × 10⁷ Pa; ε ≈ 3.5 × 10⁻⁴; x ≈ 8.7 × 10⁻⁴ m

sections.content: The single most common error in Paper 2 stress/strain calculations is using the diameter instead of the radius when finding cross-sectional area. Always halve the diameter first, then apply A = πr². If you use d in the formula directly you will be out by a factor of 4 — a whole mark gone. Also, if a question asks you to 'define' the Young modulus, write it as a ratio (stress divided by strain) AND state it applies only within the limit of proportionality. One-line definitions that omit the proportionality condition often lose a mark.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What is the difference between stress and strain in Cambridge 9702 Physics?
faq.a: Stress (σ = F/A) measures the internal force per unit cross-sectional area in pascals, while strain (ε = x/L₀) measures the fractional change in length and has no units. They are related through the Young modulus E = σ/ε. Our free 9702 course covers both definitions with worked examples and past-paper practice questions so you can master the distinction quickly.

faq.q: How do I calculate the Young modulus from a stress–strain graph in A Level Physics?
faq.a: The Young modulus equals the gradient of the straight-line (linear) portion of the stress–strain graph — the region before the limit of proportionality. Choose two well-separated points on the straight section, calculate rise/run (Δσ/Δε), and the result is E in pascals. If you have a force–extension graph instead, you need to multiply the gradient by L₀/A to convert it to E. Our premium lessons walk through this step-by-step with real past-paper data.

faq.q: What are the key points on a stress–strain graph I need to know for 9702 Paper 2?
faq.a: You need to identify and describe four key features: (1) the limit of proportionality, where the linear relationship between stress and strain ends; (2) the elastic limit, beyond which permanent deformation occurs; (3) the yield point, where large plastic strain occurs at roughly constant stress; and (4) the ultimate tensile stress (UTS), the maximum stress before necking and fracture. Examiners regularly ask you to mark these on a sketch graph, so practise drawing and labelling it from memory. Find specimen and past-paper questions on all of these in our free 9702 revision resources.
